topic: A person learns their childhood best friend doesn't remember them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The laughter started easily, a familiar, bright sound that used to echo in my ears whenever I looked at Liam. Now, it was directed solely at Maya. She leaned in, recounting a story about a weekend trip, her eyes sparkling with an easy affection I hadn't seen directed at me in years. My stomach tightened, a cold, heavy knot forming beneath my ribs. I watched the way she laughed, the unguarded ease in her posture, and a sharp, unwelcome sensation prickled behind my eyes. I forced a smile, a brittle thing that didn't reach my mouth. "That sounds amazing, Maya," I managed, the words sounding thin and strained. Liam, sitting beside me, offered a polite nod, his attention already drifting back to his phone. The silence that followed felt vast, amplifying the small, internal friction building within me. I picked at a loose thread on my sleeve, my fingers suddenly feeling clumsy and restless.

[story 2]

I caught sight of them across the crowded cafe. Liam was animated, recounting an anecdote from high school, his hand gesturing emphatically as he spoke. Maya listened, her head tilted, a soft, sympathetic expression on her face. Every shared glance between them felt like a physical blow, a sudden drop in my chest. I shifted in my seat, pulling my jacket tighter around myself, suddenly hyper-aware of the way my own hands were clenched in my lap. When Liam finally turned to include me in the conversation, his smile was warm, genuine, and entirely devoid of the shared history I cherished. "So, that reminds me," he said, his tone light, "we should totally check out that new arcade downtown." The suggestion hung in the air, a clear invitation to an activity I wasn't invited to. I nodded quickly, the movement too sharp, and felt a distinct heat rising in my cheeks, a frantic need to prove my own presence mattered, even when it clearly didn't.

[story 3]

It was the way Liam looked at her. A soft, lingering gaze that spoke of a deep, comfortable connection, a bond I distinctly felt excluded from. I tried to engage, launching into a story about a recent project, injecting as much enthusiasm as I could muster. But his responses were brief, polite acknowledgments, his eyes flicking away as if the subject wasn't interesting. The air around us seemed to thin, leaving me feeling exposed and oddly hollowed out. I watched Maya lean in, her response immediate and flowing, a seamless exchange that felt like a closed circuit I was no longer part of. A tightness seized my throat, making it difficult to swallow. I gripped my coffee mug so hard my knuckles turned white, wishing, with a sudden, sharp pang, that I could somehow pull that easy camaraderie back into my orbit.
